build_check.description: The project launches with the headless command, exits with code 0, and produces no fatal errors. Returns 0 or 1; the rest of the score is multiplied by this gate.

requirements.description: During investigation, the player clicks hotspots at locations to gather evidence cards. Each card has a fact, source, and relevance tag. At least four evidence cards are collectible. Score 0 if there is no investigation phase, if hotspots do not yield evidence, or if fewer than three cards exist. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

During debates, suspects make statements displayed as text panels. The player identifies contradictions and fires evidence cards at contradicting statements. A correct hit breaks the statement with a dramatic effect. Score 0 if there is no debate phase, if evidence cannot be presented against statements, or if hits have no effect. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Incorrect evidence presentation costs reputation points. A reputation meter is visible. Losing all reputation fails the debate. The penalty creates real risk for guessing. Score 0 if wrong answers have no penalty, if reputation does not exist, or if failure is impossible. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

After breaking a contradiction, the debate advances to a new phase with harder claims and new information revealed. At least two debate phases exist per suspect. Score 0 if the debate ends after one contradiction or if no progression exists. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

The full loop connects: title -> investigation -> debate rounds -> final accusation -> verdict result. Both successful verdict and failed debate are reachable outcomes with styled result screens. Score 0 if any transition is missing or if one outcome is unreachable. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

At least three suspects participate in debates with different personalities, claim styles, and contradictions. Each suspect requires different evidence to break. Score 0 if fewer than two suspects exist or if all suspects are identical. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Investigation locations vary (at least three: classroom, library, courtyard or equivalent) with different visual settings and evidence to find. Score 0 if only one location exists or if locations are identical. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Debate statements are numerous enough that the player must read and compare before identifying the contradiction — not a single obvious lie. At least four statements per debate round with only one or two being contradictable. Score 0 if contradictions are trivially obvious. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

The final round requires identifying the true culprit from accumulated evidence across all debates. This is a distinct deduction challenge, not just another contradiction break. Score 0 if there is no final accusation or if the culprit is obvious without deduction. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Evidence cards have enough variety and specificity that the player must choose carefully — presenting the wrong evidence (even if related) fails. At least six distinct evidence cards exist. Score 0 if fewer than four cards exist or if any card works on any contradiction. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Normal startup shows a styled title screen with academic-thriller atmosphere (school setting, investigation motif). Score 0 if it launches into raw gameplay or default UI. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

The flow connects title -> investigation -> debate -> verdict without restarting. Phase transitions are clear. The player always knows whether they are investigating or debating. Score 0 if transitions are broken or if phases are indistinguishable. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Statements, evidence cards, reputation meter, and available actions are readable at 1280x720 without overlap. During debates, the current statement and evidence selection are clear. Score 0 if the player cannot determine what to do. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Key moments have visible feedback: finding evidence shows a collection effect, correct hits show statement-breaking animation, wrong hits show penalty effect, and the verdict has dramatic staging. Score 0 if important events have no visual feedback. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Overall art coherence: school locations, character portraits, evidence cards, debate stage, and UI belong to a consistent academic-thriller visual language. Score 0 if the screen mixes incompatible styles or reads as plain default Godot grey. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

Character portraits are real illustrated or pixel-art figures with distinct appearances and expressions that change during debates. Suspects react visibly when caught in contradictions. Score 0 if characters have no portraits or are static shapes. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

Location backgrounds and debate stage are real illustrated environments with school-appropriate detail. Evidence cards are styled objects with illustrated frames and icons. Score 0 if backgrounds are flat colors or if cards are plain text. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

UI uses styled academic-thriller chrome: evidence tray has case-file styling, reputation meter has themed design, statement panels have debate-podium framing, and transitions use dramatic effects (statement shatter, spotlight). Score 0 if UI elements are unstyled Godot defaults. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.
